nativeRange.habitat: Shallow marine environments including inner reef flats, sheltered lagoons, and algal or silty reefs; also found among attached Sargassum on shallow rubble flats at depths of 1–15 m

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding primarily on filamentous green algae and to a lesser extent on fleshy and coralline red algae, as well as benthic invertebrates. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of mysis shrimp, krill, chopped clams, and other meaty foods; algae-based foods and vegetable matter are generally advised to reflect the species' natural herbivorous leanings. Hard-shelled prey such as clams on the half shell or small snails are recommended periodically to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like fused teeth.

summary: A small Indo-West Pacific toby puffer reaching roughly 10 cm; tends to inhabit shallow reef flats and lagoons. Generally kept singly in fish-only or FOWLR systems; has been known to nip invertebrates and corals.
careNotes:
Canthigaster bennetti (Bleeker, 1854) is a widely distributed sharpnose puffer of the family Tetraodontidae, ranging across the Indo-West Pacific from East Africa south to Port Alfred (South Africa) and east to the Tuamotu Islands, with records north to southern Taiwan and south to New South Wales, Australia. A photo-documented population has also been noted in the Southeast Atlantic.

In the wild, this species tends to inhabit inner reef flats and sheltered lagoons, and is commonly reported on algal or silty reefs and among attached Sargassum on shallow rubble flats at depths of 1–15 m. FishBase records a preferred temperature range of 25.1–29.3°C (mean ~28.4°C) for wild populations; the aquarium range of 24–28°C used here reflects these wild parameters while aligning with the practical range commonly applied to Indo-Pacific reef fish in hobbyist settings.

This species is one of the smaller Canthigaster tobies, with a maximum total length reported at 10.0 cm. Coloration is typically brownish to olive dorsally with white or pale undersides and scattered blue-lined spots; like most tobies it lacks an obvious lateral line but is well-adapted to reef environments.

Its diet in the wild is predominantly herbivorous — primarily filamentous green algae — supplemented by benthic invertebrates including small crustaceans and molluscs. In captivity it has generally been reported to accept a mixed omnivore diet of algae-based foods and meaty items. The species' beak-like fused teeth grow continuously and benefit from periodic offerings of hard-shelled prey.

FishBase designates the species as monogamous in its reproductive system. Males are typically territorial and are best kept one per tank; conspecific and intrageneric aggression toward other Canthigaster tobies have been reported in hobbyist accounts. Fin-nipping tendencies toward long-finned tankmates and consumption of small ornamental invertebrates are characteristic of the genus.

Like all tetraodontid puffers, Canthigaster bennetti contains tetrodotoxin in its skin, viscera and gonads. Under severe stress or at death there is a small risk of toxin release into the aquarium water, which can harm sensitive tankmates. The species should be transported using a water-filled container rather than a net to prevent defensive inflation, as inhaling air can be harmful.

No dedicated aquarium retail page for this species was located during research (LiveAquaria puffers collection did not list it; Reef2Reef and BlueZoo returned 403 or no content). Care parameters have been cross-verified against the closely related C. valentini (Bleeker, 1853) and C. margaritata, both of which are well-documented Canthigaster species of comparable size and ecology; FishBase wild data for C. bennetti itself served as the primary cross-verification source for size and temperature.

Single-source concern: no dedicated hobbyist aquarium care page for C. bennetti was accessible; all aquarium-specific parameters (pH, salinity, tank size) are inferred from FishBase wild data and consensus across closely related Canthigaster species. Review recommended if a dedicated care source becomes available.
breedingNotes: FishBase records this species as oviparous and monogamous. Wild spawning in Canthigaster species typically involves male territory defence and demersal egg attachment in algal tufts; no widely documented captive-breeding protocol for C. bennetti is known, and breeding in home aquariums is generally considered impractical.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: The LiveAquaria puffers collection was fetched but did not list Canthigaster bennetti. 22 puffer species were listed without C. bennetti appearing among them. No dedicated LiveAquaria product page for this species was locatable. LiveAquaria served as the designated primary source per the manifest; because no species-specific page was available, FishBase was elevated to primary verification source and other Canthigaster species pages on LiveAquaria were used for care parameter consensus.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Primary data source for species identification. Confirmed: scientific name Canthigaster bennetti (Bleeker, 1854); family Tetraodontidae; order Tetraodontiformes; max total length 10.0 cm; native range Indo-West Pacific (East Africa to Tuamotu Islands, Taiwan to New South Wales, Southeast Atlantic); preferred temperature 25.1–29.3°C (mean 28.4°C); depth 1–15 m; habitat inner reef flats and sheltered lagoons; diet primarily filamentous green algae supplemented by red algae and benthic invertebrates; trophic level 2.5; monogamous reproductive system; IUCN Least Concern. Salinity and pH data not provided by FishBase for this species — inferred from marine habitat designation and genus-level consensus.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Saddle Valentini Puffer (comparable Canthigaster)
additional.notes: Used for cross-reference on aquarium care parameters for the Canthigaster genus: pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.020–1.025, 30-gallon minimum tank, semi-aggressive temperament, intermediate care level, reef-safe with caution status. C. valentini is a sister species of similar maximum size (~10–11 cm) and overlapping range.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Pearl Toby Puffer (comparable Canthigaster)
additional.notes: Additional Canthigaster genus cross-reference: pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.020–1.025, 150 L minimum tank for a 10–12 cm Canthigaster. Confirmed intermediate care level and semi-aggressive temperament as genus-typical values.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding protocol; wild reproductive system is oviparous and monogamous with demersal egg attachment in algal tufts.
